title: Ensure That Cloud SQL Database Instances Do Not Implicitly  Whitelist All Public IP Addresses

input_row.title: Ensure That Cloud SQL Database Instances Do Not Implicitly  Whitelist All Public IP Addresses
input_row.description: Database Server should accept connections only from trusted Network(s)/IP(s) and restrict access from public IP addresses.
input_row.rationale: To minimize attack surface on a Database server instance, only trusted/known and required IP(s) should be white-listed to connect to it. An authorized network should not have IPs/networks configured to 0.0.0.0/0 which will allow access to the instance from anywhere in the world. Note that authorized networks apply only to instances with public IPs.
input_row.audit: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the Cloud SQL Instances page in the Google Cloud Console by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/sql/instances. 2. Click the instance name to open its Instance details page. 3. Under the Configuration section click Edit configurations 4. Under Configuration options expand the Connectivity section. 5. Ensure that no authorized network is configured to allow 0.0.0.0/0. From Google Cloud CLI 1. Get detailed configuration for every Cloud SQL database instance. gcloud sql instances list --format=json Ensure that the section settings: ipConfiguration : authorizedNetworks does not have any parameter value containing 0.0.0.0/0.
input_row.remediation: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the Cloud SQL Instances page in the Google Cloud Console by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/sql/instances. 2. Click the instance name to open its Instance details page. 3. Under the Configuration section click Edit configurations 4. Under Configuration options expand the Connectivity section. 5. Click the delete icon for the authorized network 0.0.0.0/0. 6. Click Save to update the instance. From Google Cloud CLI Update the authorized network list by dropping off any addresses. gcloud sql instances patch --authorized- networks=IP_ADDR1,IP_ADDR2... Prevention: To prevent new SQL instances from being configured to accept incoming connections from any IP addresses, set up a Restrict Authorized Networks on Cloud SQL instances Organization Policy at: https://console.cloud.google.com/iam- admin/orgpolicies/sql-restrictAuthorizedNetworks.
input_row.impact: The Cloud SQL database instance would not be available to public IP addresses.
input_row.default_value: By default, authorized networks are not configured. Remote connection to Cloud SQL database instance is not possible unless authorized networks are configured.
input_row.additional_information: There is no IPv6 configuration found for Google cloud SQL server services.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_sql_instance_authorized_networks_not_whitelisted

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:** 
1. Use Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) to list all Cloud SQL instances with their configurations:
   - Command: `gcloud sql instances list --format=json`
   - This will retrieve all instances along with their associated configurations.
  
2. Inspect the `ipConfiguration` section of each instance:
   - Check the `authorizedNetworks` field within the `ipConfiguration` for the presence of `0.0.0.0/0`.
   
3. Pass/Fail criteria:
   - **Pass:** No authorized network entry should be `0.0.0.0/0` for any Cloud SQL instance.
   - **Fail:** At least one authorized network entry is `0.0.0.0/0`, indicating that the instance allows connections from any public IP.

4. Additionally, reference the following documentation links for further verification and understanding of policies:
   - [Configure IP for Cloud SQL](https://cloud.google.com/sql/docs/mysql/configure-ip)
   - [Restrict Authorized Networks Policy](https://console.cloud.google.com/iam-admin/orgpolicies/sql-restrictAuthorizedNetworks)
   - [Organization Policy Constraints](https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/organization-policy/org-policy-constraints)
   - [Connection through Organization Policy](https://cloud.google.com/sql/docs/mysql/connection-org-policy)

This automated program will ensure compliance by regularly checking the configurations of Cloud SQL instances and identifying any instances that are misconfigured to allow public access.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE  
- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is correct. The task of checking Cloud SQL instance configurations for public IP whitelisting can be effectively automated using the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) and its CLI commands.  
- The use of `gcloud sql instances list --format=json` is appropriate for retrieving the necessary configuration details of Cloud SQL instances, which can then be parsed to check for the presence of `0.0.0.0/0` in the `authorizedNetworks` field.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 9  
- The automation details are technically accurate. The command `gcloud sql instances list --format=json` is valid and will retrieve the necessary data to check the IP configurations of Cloud SQL instances.  
- The Pass/Fail criteria are correctly defined, focusing on the presence of `0.0.0.0/0` in the `authorizedNetworks` field.  
- The program name `gcp_sql_instance_authorized_networks_not_whitelisted` is descriptive and follows a logical naming convention, although it could be slightly more specific by including terms like 'public_ip' to clarify the exact security check being performed.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**  
- **Program Name Suggestion:** Consider renaming the program to `gcp_sql_instance_public_ip_whitelist_check` for more clarity on the specific security check being performed.  
- **Additional Considerations:** It might be beneficial to include a check for whether the Cloud SQL instances have public IPs enabled at all, as disabling public IPs can be a stronger security measure.  
- **Alternative Methods:** While the current method is effective, consider using Google Cloud's Asset Inventory API for a more comprehensive asset management approach, which could provide additional context or metadata about the instances.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH  
- The assessment is well-founded, and the use of Google Cloud SDK commands is appropriate for the task. The automation logic is sound and aligns with GCP security best practices.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:** Modify  
- While the overall approach and technical details are solid, I recommend modifying the program name for greater specificity and clarity. Additionally, consider incorporating checks for public IP enablement as part of a more comprehensive security assessment.
